name: e2e-testing-patterns
description: Master end-to-end testing with Playwright and Cypress to build reliable test suites that catch bugs, improve confidence, and enable fast deployment. Use when implementing E2E tests, debugging flaky tests, or establishing testing standards.
capabilities: Implement Page Object Model for test maintainability
Create test fixtures for reliable test data management
Debug failing tests with trace viewer and screenshots
Set up CI/CD pipelines with parallel test execution
Mock API responses for faster, deterministic tests
Write accessible tests using ARIA roles and attributes
author: sickn33
securitySummary: All 63 static findings are false positives. The analyzer incorrectly interprets markdown code fences as Ruby shell commands, JavaScript operators as cryptographic issues, and test configuration as system reconnaissance. This is a documentation skill with educational content about E2E testing patterns using Playwright and Cypress. No actual security risks exist.
valueStatement: Writing end-to-end tests is challenging. This skill provides battle-tested patterns for Playwright and Cypress to create stable, maintainable tests that catch bugs before users do.
limitations: This skill provides patterns and examples, not a test runner itself
Does not cover unit testing or integration testing strategies
Requires existing Playwright or Cypress project to apply patterns

useCases.title: Set Up E2E Tests for New Project
useCases.description: Configure Playwright or Cypress from scratch with proper structure, reporters, and CI integration

useCases.targetUser: QA engineers dealing with unreliable test suites
useCases.title: Fix Flaky Tests
useCases.description: Replace brittle selectors and fixed timeouts with proper waiting strategies and stable locators

useCases.title: Scale Test Execution
useCases.description: Implement parallel execution and test sharding to reduce CI pipeline time from hours to minutes

promptTemplates.title: Create Login Test
promptTemplates.prompt: Create an end-to-end test for a login page using Playwright. Include proper selectors, assertions, and error handling for both successful and failed login attempts.

promptTemplates.title: Mock API Response
promptTemplates.prompt: Show how to mock an API response in Cypress to test error handling without relying on a real backend service.
promptTemplates.scenario: Testing error states in checkout flow

promptTemplates.title: Set Up CI Pipeline
promptTemplates.prompt: Configure Playwright to run tests in CI with parallel execution, retry logic, and HTML report generation.

promptTemplates.title: Accessibility Testing
promptTemplates.prompt: Implement accessibility testing using axe-core to ensure the application meets WCAG guidelines.

bestPractices: Use data-testid or semantic roles instead of CSS selectors for stable locators
Keep tests independent with proper setup and teardown of test data
Run tests in parallel in CI to reduce execution time
antiPatterns: Using fixed timeouts like waitForTimeout(3000) - causes flakiness
Testing implementation details instead of user-visible behavior
Sharing state between tests - leads to cascade failures

faq.question: Playwright vs Cypress: Which should I choose?
faq.answer: Playwright offers better cross-browser support and faster execution. Cypress has a more mature ecosystem and easier debugging. Choose based on your browser requirements and team familiarity.

faq.question: How do I make tests less flaky?
faq.answer: Use explicit waits instead of timeouts, avoid testing implementation details, ensure test data is properly isolated, and add retry logic for network-dependent operations.

faq.question: How do I run tests in parallel?
faq.answer: Configure Playwright workers or Cypress parallelization. Use test sharding to split tests across multiple machines in CI. Ensure tests are independent to avoid conflicts.

faq.question: Can I test APIs with E2E tools?
faq.answer: Yes, both Playwright and Cypress support API testing. Use page.request() in Playwright or cy.request() in Cypress for direct API calls without UI interaction.

faq.question: How do I handle authentication in tests?
faq.answer: Use API login to set cookies or tokens, or use browser storage APIs to set session data directly. Avoid UI login in every test for better performance.

faq.question: What is the Page Object Model?
faq.answer: A design pattern that encapsulates page-specific logic and selectors in a class. Improves test maintainability by centralizing page changes in one place rather than across all tests.
